content:
A few months ago, everyone was talking about vibe coding.
Open an AI coding tool.
Describe an idea.
Watch it generate an application.
Ship it.
Repeat.
It felt magical.
For the first time, people without years of programming experience could build websites, SaaS products, landing pages, and even full-stack applications in hours instead of weeks.
But something interesting happened.
The internet became filled with products that looked… exactly the same.
The same gradients.
The same rounded buttons.
The same hero sections.
The same AI-generated copy.
The same feature cards.
The same promise to “revolutionize productivity.”
At first, it was exciting.
Today, it’s becoming forgettable.
And that’s why I believe the era of vibe coding is already coming to an end.
Not because AI stopped improving.
But because everyone started using it in the exact same way.

AI Didn’t Remove Creativity
It Removed Friction.
That’s an important difference.
Before AI, building software required significant effort.
Ideas had to survive weeks — or months — of development before they reached users.
Today, someone can go from idea to MVP in a weekend.
That’s incredible.
But lower friction creates a new problem.
More products.
More noise.
More competition.
The challenge is no longer building software.
The challenge is building something people actually remember.

Design Isn’t Decoration
One thing AI still struggles with is intentional design.
A beautiful interface isn’t just a collection of modern components.
Good design communicates.
It creates trust.
It establishes personality.
It guides attention.
It makes products memorable.
Two companies can sell the exact same service.
The one with a stronger identity usually wins.
Not because the software is better.
Because people remember it.

The Real Competitive Advantage Is No Longer Code
This may sound strange coming from a developer.
But writing code is becoming less valuable as a competitive advantage.
Don’t misunderstand.
Developers are more important than ever.
But the value has shifted.
Tomorrow’s successful builders won’t simply be people who can generate code.
They’ll be people who know:

• Which problems are worth solving.

• What users actually need.

• How to create memorable experiences.

• How to simplify complexity.

• How to build trust.
AI can generate components.
It can’t generate genuine understanding.

Branding Is Making a Comeback
For years, startups competed on speed.
Now everyone is fast.
The next wave belongs to products with personality.
People remember brands that feel human.
Not because they have the fanciest animations.
But because they tell a story.
They have opinions.
They sound different.
They solve specific problems exceptionally well.
In a world of AI-generated sameness, authenticity becomes a competitive advantage.

What Comes Next?
I think we’re entering a new era.
An era where AI becomes the baseline instead of the advantage.
The winners won’t be the people using the most AI.
They’ll be the people combining AI with creativity, taste, curiosity, and deep customer understanding.
AI will handle repetition.
Humans will define direction.
That’s a much more interesting future than replacing developers altogether.

Final Thoughts
For a while, it felt like the ability to build software was the ultimate superpower.
Today, that superpower is becoming accessible to everyone.
And that’s a good thing.
Because it shifts our focus toward what has always mattered most.
Not how quickly we can build.
But what we choose to build — and why anyone should care.
The era of vibe coding isn’t ending because AI failed.
It’s ending because AI became ordinary.
The next generation of great products won’t win by looking like everyone else.
They’ll win by being impossible to forget.
